video_title: Bitcoin Money Is Rotating — Here's Where It's Going
gist: Former crypto hedge fund manager Avi Felman explains why he has divested nearly all Bitcoin: with genuine secular tech growth (AI, robotics, biotech, defense), Bitcoin's escape-hatch narrative has lost relevance, capital is rotating into equities, and BTC faces specific overhangs (Michael Sailor/MSTR, quantum, no narrative). He lays out where he is deploying instead and how retail-driven volatility is reshaping every market.

themes.theme: Bitcoin Divestment Thesis and Rotation Out of Crypto

insights.claim: Bitcoin's core value proposition as an escape hatch from Fed/government mistakes only matters when the Fed has massive control over the economy, and that relevance is fading because genuine secular growth in AI, robotics, biotech and defense now takes a backseat to monetary irresponsibility.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: The macro narrative that drove BTC demand is structurally weakening as real-economy growth dominates attention.
insights.quote: Bitcoin is valuable because if the system makes mistakes, there's an escape hatch. But that only makes sense in a world where the Fed has massive control.

insights.claim: Avi Felman, in crypto since 2016/2017 and formerly ~80% allocated, now owns no Bitcoin and almost no crypto, having fully divested because attention has shifted elsewhere and crypto lacks a current narrative to drive purchases.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: A former deep crypto insider capitulating on holdings signals a broader sentiment and flow shift out of the asset class.
insights.quote: It used to be 80% of my portfolio... today it just doesn't make a ton of sense to be holding this drag when I can be holding memory stocks.

insights.claim: Crypto will follow the internet-bubble arc: the label disappears and surviving projects become revenue-generating 'fintech companies that use crypto as a back end' rather than 'crypto companies'.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Invest in crypto projects by revenue and users, not by infrastructure/token narratives.
insights.quote: We're no longer going to have crypto companies in the same sense. We're going to have fintech companies that use crypto as a back end.

insights.claim: The only crypto Felman still holds long-term is privacy coins Zcash and a little Monero, as a hedge against future wealth taxes or capital seizure driven by rising socialism.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Privacy coins are framed as the specific crypto hedge for a political-seizure scenario, not BTC.
insights.quote: I own Zcash and a little bit of Monero as well because I think that it's important to have these things as a hedge.

themes.theme: The Sailor / MSTR Overhang and Conditions to Re-buy Bitcoin

insights.claim: Institutional and traditional-finance investors are avoiding Bitcoin primarily over three fears: Michael Sailor/Strategy, quantum computing, and the absence of any narrative to drive buying.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Named catalysts must resolve before institutional flows return to BTC.
insights.quote: They're worried about Sailor. They're worried about two things. They're worried about quantum and they're worried about Sailor.

insights.claim: Felman will re-buy Bitcoin only when three conditions are met — the Sailor problem is solved, the quantum problem is solved, and Fed liquidity injection revives inflation fears — or on a capitulation puke to around $40K.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Defines a concrete BTC re-entry level (~$40K) and the macro/idiosyncratic triggers to watch.
insights.quote: If we get like a puke out, if we trade 40K, I'll probably get back in. But until then, I'm waiting for these three things.

insights.claim: Strategy's 'Stretch' Bitcoin savings vehicle is trading around 76 (below par), and Sailor has the option to simply turn off the non-smart-contract dividend, nuking Stretch holders to zero while keeping all the Bitcoin.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Stretch holders bear pausable dividend risk; Sailor's incentive is to protect BTC and MSTR, not preferred holders.
insights.quote: This morning when I checked Stretch it's at 76... The dividend is not in a smart contract. He can pause it at any time.

insights.claim: Felman thinks it is probable, not just possible, that Sailor lets Stretch go to zero by shutting off the dividend, because that is easier than fighting to the 'dirty end' and risking MSTR going to zero via dilutive at-the-money offerings.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Preferred/Stretch exposure carries a materially probable wipeout scenario per a former distressed-credit operator.
insights.quote: Not only that, it's probable.

insights.claim: The Stretch/MSTR situation is not a Luna analogue because Luna had a reflexive UST-to-Luna arbitrage that printed infinite supply to zero, a mechanism that does not exist here.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Rejects the popular Luna comparison; the failure mode is a policy choice, not an automated death spiral.
insights.quote: It can't be a Luna... there was a continuous arb to just print infinite Luna and send it to zero. You can't do that with [Stretch].

insights.claim: If Strategy is forced to sell, Sailor likely only needs to sell roughly 15–25% of total Bitcoin to cover all debt (converts and everything), and any such forced selling is a signal to buy BTC as aggressively as possible.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Quantifies downside supply overhang and frames forced liquidation as a buying opportunity.
insights.quote: Whenever there's a forced seller in the market, you need to step in there and buy as much as you possibly can.

insights.claim: The Hyperliquid thesis is essentially the Robinhood/Kalshi/Polymarket thesis on the financialization of the world — people now trade everything — and Felman classifies Hyperliquid as a revenue-generating fintech company with multiple potential winners in a large market.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Own the financialization rails by revenue; the TAM supports several winners, not a single one.
insights.quote: The hyperliquid thesis is actually basically the same thesis as the Robin Hood thesis... the market is big enough that there are going to be multiple winners.

insights.claim: A broken social contract — pensions gone, 401k returns eroded by inflation and runaway housing/asset prices captured by the capital class — is driving average Americans to gamble and trade (sports betting, weather, Kalshi, Polymarket) to escape a 'permanent underclass'.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Structural demographic/economic despair is a durable tailwind for trading and prediction-market volumes.
insights.quote: The social contract in many ways has been broken so people are searching for ways to escape... they're gambling on sports, they're betting on the weather.

insights.claim: Traditional exchanges (NYSE, NASDAQ) are moving toward prediction markets and integrating gambling because that is where the world is going, and the GameStop/COVID day-trading era created a lasting addiction to being on the winning side.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Incumbent exchanges converging with prediction/gambling venues validates the sector's expansion.
insights.quote: You're actually seeing the New York Stock Exchange and NASDAQ... they're integrating the gambling. They're integrating all that because they realize that's where the world is going.

insights.claim: Felman is bullish on other revenue-generating crypto projects including Hyperliquid, Courtyard/Cards (trading-card collectibles), and Eric Voorhees's VVV, on the criterion that they generate real revenue regardless of debated token value accrual.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Revenue and users are the new screening filter for crypto token selection.
insights.quote: If you look at VVV, which is Eric Vorhees's project... the thing is making money. It's actually pulling in revenue.

themes.theme: The AI-Driven Rotation: Memory, Downstream Trades and Sector Bets

insights.claim: The Mag 7 crowded trade has been overtaken by memory stocks because hyperscalers spending ~$600 billion on data centers must buy memory, while their equity issuance depresses their own share prices — a shift from getting ahead to a game of competitive survival.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Capex flows explain the memory-stock leadership rotation away from mega-cap tech.
insights.quote: If they're going to be building out $600 billion of data centers... that money is going to memory.

insights.claim: With memory now in a 'crazy bubble,' the next trade is what AI will actually change downstream: short the consultants (Accenture down 30% in a day), buy biotech (XBI, Cathie Wood's ARKG), and buy defense benefiting from smart forward-deployed drones.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Actionable rotation: pair short consulting against long biotech/defense as AI's second-order beneficiaries.
insights.quote: Maybe short the consultant, buy biotech.

insights.claim: Felman runs a barbell of 'what AI will affect' and 'American isolationism / competition with China,' making him a big Intel bull versus TSMC — expecting Intel (~$600B cap) to close the gap with TSMC (~$3T) because domestic chip capacity is a national-security imperative akin to the Manhattan Project.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: US chip reshoring is a structural, policy-backed long for Intel over Taiwan-exposed TSMC.
insights.quote: That's why I'm still a huge believer in Intel. Intel is at 600 billion market cap. TSMC, which is based in Taiwan, is at a 3 trillion. They're going to close that gap.

insights.claim: Felman is a big uranium bull, holding uranium miners rather than the commodity, because even if uranium prices fall on oversupply, miners selling far higher volumes still see multiples of revenue growth amid a Trump-administration nuclear push.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Express nuclear/energy conviction through miners for volume-driven revenue, not the underlying commodity price.
insights.quote: If they're selling 10x and price is down 50%, they're still 5x up on their revenue. And so I hold uranium miners as well.

insights.claim: SpaceX at ~$1.8T trades near 100x price-to-sales on ~$18B revenue, but Felman is a buyer under $1 trillion, arguing it is the only viable space company and could grow into its valuation like Nvidia, which traded at 45x P/S in 2023 with only a 10% max drawdown before falling to 22x.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Sets a specific SpaceX entry (<$1T) and uses Nvidia's multiple compression as the bull template.
insights.quote: I'm a buyer of SpaceX under a trillion for sure, 100%.

insights.claim: Stablecoins will 'eat the world' but that does not mean Coinbase and Circle win — Circle could be in trouble as banks fight to buy time to issue their own stablecoins, while Tether is winning across the board yet is not investable.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Bullish thesis on stablecoins does not translate to the tradeable equities; Circle faces competitive risk.
insights.quote: Stable coins are going to eat the world, but that does not mean Coinbase and Circle are going to do well... I actually think that Circle could be in some trouble.

themes.theme: How to Generate Edge and Read Panic

insights.claim: Edge comes not from macro prognostication ('astrology for men') but from identifying what people don't understand or aren't looking at — e.g., buying memory before the market grasped post-ChatGPT memory demand.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Focus research on informational gaps, not rate/VIX/skew tea-leaves, to generate returns.
insights.quote: A lot of macro talk is kind of just astrology for men.

insights.claim: Buffett's 'blood on the streets' means buying only when participants are truly panicking and selling good companies at irrational prices — not merely when the market is down 10-15% for rational reasons like repricing tariffs.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Distinguish rational selloffs from genuine panic to time contrarian buying correctly.
insights.quote: He means buy when people are truly panicking... they're selling good stocks, good companies at irrational prices because they are fully panicked.

insights.claim: Felman's best-ever trade was buying Ripple equity off the FTX estate; when FTX collapsed, SBF personally called him at Golden Tree seeking $2-3B to cover what was actually an ~$8B hole, illustrating that peak-fear counterparties act irrationally.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Distressed opportunities cluster around panicked, irrational sellers at crisis moments.
insights.quote: The best trade that I've ever taken in my entire life was buying Ripple equity off of the FTX estate.

insights.claim: In high-volatility regimes cash becomes more valuable; Felman allocates to mega-trends by buying on extreme downside moves (~30% collapses every few quarters) and selling or writing calls into insane upside spikes, because high implied vol raises the odds of revisiting entry prices.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: A volatility-harvesting playbook: buy dips, trim/sell calls on spikes, hold dry powder.
insights.quote: When V is this high you get presented with opportunities basically every 3 months where things collapse 30%.

themes.theme: Retail, AI-Compressed Expertise and Bubble Signals

insights.claim: AI compresses the time to become 'expert enough' to trade a theme, letting retail instantly surface names (e.g., asking Claude for AI-trade beneficiaries), which Felman argues is why SK Hynix rose as far as it did versus a hedge-fund-only move in the past.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: AI-democratized research amplifies retail crowding into narratives, intensifying single-stock moves.
insights.quote: Now you go into Claude and you type in what are the biggest beneficiaries from the AI trade and all these companies populate.

insights.claim: Because capital and information now move at the speed of light, everything is far more volatile, producing a K-shaped bifurcation in market outcomes; Pomp's Sylvia AI CFO users grew net worth 16-40% in six months across all income and starting-net-worth levels.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: Heightened volatility rewards good positioning and punishes bad, widening the winner/loser gap even in personal finance.
insights.quote: If you use Sylvia regularly or heavily your net worth has grown 16 to 40% in the last 6 months... across all income levels.

insights.claim: In today's public markets the herd is more right than not because X functions as a global chat room of many smart, well-informed participants — so edge is less about pure contrarianism and more about being online enough to catch narratives early and 'get in the way' of them.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: Reflexively fading crowded public-market narratives is often wrong; front-running them pays better.
insights.quote: The herd is actually more right than not in the public market.

insights.claim: Bubble tops are identified by building a mosaic of overexuberance signals — taxi drivers discussing Intel, doctor friends starting to trade, Korean leverage ratios (up 3x YTD) approaching ~$50B in SK Hynix — rather than one silver bullet.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Concrete crowd-sentiment and leverage thresholds to watch for signaling the AI-trade top.
insights.quote: If a taxi driver starts talking to me about Intel, I'm probably selling out.

insights.claim: Felman recounts the crypto-cycle top signal: a multibillionaire non-crypto investor showing off a ~$1.1B crypto position built off a dinner tip, then day-trading Dogecoin in an Uber — a mosaic of euphoria that meant 'there can't be much juice left'.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Extreme late-stage retail/whale euphoria across unexpected participants marks cycle tops.
insights.quote: He flips from the Uber app to Robin Hood... I go, 'What are you looking at?' He goes, 'Dogecoin.' And I should have immediately got on my computer and sold everything.

insights.claim: Meme-stock moves (Wendy's, GameStop) are driven by shared identity and the fun of making money together rather than fundamentals — Wendy's spiked ~30% off a Reddit joke about saving jobs — and cults now surround tickers like INTC, SanDisk and Hyperliquid.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Understanding the social-identity meme mechanism is now essential to trading crypto-fied equity markets.
insights.quote: Whenever you get a meme stock that runs, it's because... it's so much more fun to make money together.

themes.theme: Private Markets, NAV Premiums and Access Vehicles

insights.claim: Publicly traded closed-end funds holding private companies often carry justified NAV premiums because marks are stuck at the last funding round; the danger signal is not absolute premium level but rapid NAV expansion (e.g., 200% to 500% in two days) without a corresponding company event.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Evaluate premium-to-NAV vehicles by the speed and justification of NAV changes, not the headline premium.
insights.quote: The actual absolute number [is] almost irrelevant... what makes me nervous is well, the NAV just went from 200% to 500% in 2 days.

insights.claim: Index-fund forward returns may be structurally lower because companies now stay private until $1T+ valuations, letting a small cabal of private investors capture gains that used to accrue to public retail — making thematic access vehicles (RoboStrategy, Fundrise, AngelList, Robinhood's VC vehicle) valuable.
insights.speaker: Avi Felman
insights.so_what: Prolonged private-market capture is a headwind for passive index returns and a tailwind for retail-access vehicles.
insights.quote: Companies can wait until they're 1.5 trillion to go public. And they're actually taking those returns from your average American.

insights.claim: The competitive edge in private markets can favor going with top VCs (Sequoia, a16z, Benchmark) over lone-wolf contrarianism because their capital, network and recruiting materially raise a startup's probability of success — 'do you want to be the hero or do you want to make money'.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano
insights.so_what: Co-investing alongside dominant VCs can beat pure contrarian sourcing on risk-adjusted odds.
insights.quote: How many venture funds would have been better off not investing in 20 different companies and just put all their money in SpaceX.
